quality.rationale: Differential geometry proof linking axiomatic exterior derivative to coordinate-based antisymmetrized covariant derivative via tensor component analysis.

origin.answer: \mathbf{d}\boldsymbol{\Omega}$ has the same components in an arbitrary coordinate basis under both definitions, specifically $\Omega_{[\alpha \beta \dot s \gamma,\delta]}$, thus proving their equivalence.
origin.solution:
Solution 8.5. We show that the two definitions are equivalent by showing that $\mathbf{d}\Omega$ has the same components in an arbitrary coordinate basis when calculated from either definition. Start with the definition in terms of the covariant derivative. Let the components of $\Omega$ be $\Omega_{\alpha  eta \dot s \gamma} = \Omega_{[\alpha  eta \dot s \gamma]}$ ( $\mathfrak{p}$ indices). Then the components of $\mathbf{d}\Omega$ are
$$ egin{array}{l} \Omega_{[\alpha  eta \dot s \gamma ; \delta]} = \Omega_{[\alpha  eta \dot s \gamma , \delta]} - \Gamma_{\epsilon [\alpha \delta} \Omega_{ eta}^{\epsilon} \dot s \gamma ] 	ag {1} 
- \Gamma_{\epsilon [ eta \delta} \Omega_{a}^{\epsilon} \dot s \gamma - \dot s - \Gamma_{\epsilon [\gamma \delta} \Omega_{a  eta \dot s \epsilon ]} = \Omega_{[a}  eta \dot s \gamma , \delta ] 
\end{array}$$
(The $\Gamma$ terms vanish since $\Gamma_{\mu[
u \sigma]} = 0$ in a coordinate basis.) Now start with
$$\Omega = \Omega_{\alpha  eta \dot s \gamma} \widetilde{\mathrm{dx}}^{\alpha} \wedge \widetilde{\mathrm{dx}}^{ eta} \wedge \dot s \widetilde{\mathrm{dx}}^{\gamma}.$$
By property (ii), $\mathbf{d}$ does not disturb the summation convention, so
$$ egin{array}{l} \mathrm{d}  oldsymbol{\Omega} = (\mathrm{d} \Omega_{\alpha  eta \dot s \gamma}) \wedge (\widetilde{\mathrm{dx}}^{\alpha} \wedge \widetilde{\mathrm{dx}}^{ eta} \wedge \dot s \wedge \widetilde{\mathrm{dx}}^{\gamma}) 	ag {2} 
+ \Omega_{\alpha  eta \dot s \gamma} \mathrm{d} \left(\widetilde{\mathrm{dx}}^{\alpha} \wedge \widetilde{\mathrm{dx}}^{ eta} \wedge \dot s \wedge \widetilde{\mathrm{dx}}^{\gamma}ight), 
\end{array}$$
where we have used property (iv) with $\mathfrak{p} = 0$ since $\Omega_{\alpha  eta \dot s \gamma}$ is simply a function (zero form). Now from property (i) we know that
$$\widetilde{\mathrm{df}} = f_{, a} \widetilde{\mathrm{dx}}^{a}$$
for any zero form $f$ . From property (iii), $<\widetilde{\mathrm{df}}, \partial/\partial x^{ eta}> = <f_{,a} \widetilde{\mathrm{dx}}^{a}, \partial/\partial x^{ eta}> = f_{,b}$ ; thus $\widetilde{\mathrm{d}\Omega}_{\alpha eta\dot s\gamma} = \Omega_{\alpha eta\dot s\gamma,\delta} \widetilde{\mathrm{dx}}^{\delta}$ . The second term in Equation (2) is zero: According to property (iv) it can be reduced to a sum of terms each of which contains a ddx, which vanishes by property (v). Thus Equation (2) becomes
$$\mathbf{d}  oldsymbol{\Omega} = \Omega_{\alpha  eta \dot s \gamma , \delta} \widetilde{\mathbf{dx}}^{\delta} \wedge \widetilde{\mathbf{dx}}^{\alpha} \wedge \widetilde{\mathbf{dx}}^{ eta} \dot s \wedge \widetilde{\mathbf{dx}}^{\gamma}.$$
Since $\mathbf{d}\Omega$ is a $(p + 1)$ form (property (i)), and the wedge product is antisymmetric, the components of $\mathbf{d}\Omega$ are $\Omega_{[a eta \dot s \gamma ,\delta ]}$ as in Equation (1).
